ground_truth.title: Cryptography Explained Simply: The Concepts That Actually Matter
ground_truth.author: Ilias Abousalim
ground_truth.main_content: Cryptography solves real problems in a connected world: keeping data private, ensuring it hasn't been tampered with, and proving who sent it. Encryption is like putting your letter in a locked box—only someone with the right key can open it and read what's inside. Hashing is like taking a photo of your document that can't be reversed, creating a fixed-size unique fingerprint of any data. Digital signatures prove authenticity by encrypting a hash with a private key, allowing recipients to verify both the source and integrity of a message. Symmetric encryption uses one key for both locking and unlocking, while asymmetric encryption uses a public/private key pair—public key encrypts, private key decrypts. Key derivation functions like PBKDF2, bcrypt, and Argon2 transform weak human-memorable passwords into strong cryptographic keys through computational stretching. End-to-end encryption guarantees content privacy from servers and ISPs, but metadata such as timestamps, participants, and message frequency can still reveal communication patterns. Quantum computers threaten current cryptography through Shor's algorithm, which breaks RSA and ECC, necessitating post-quantum cryptography migration to algorithms like Kyber and Dilithium. Security requires more than just encryption—it demands secure key management, proper implementation, and awareness of threat models and potential limitations.
with: Cryptography solves real problems in a connected world: keeping data private, ensuring it hasn't been tampered with, and proving who sent it.
Encryption is like putting your letter in a locked box. Only someone with the right key can open it and read what's inside.
Hashing is like taking a photo of your document that can't be reversed. It's a one-way process that creates a fixed-size 'summary' of any data.
Symmetric Encryption (Shared Secret) uses one key for both locking and unlocking. Like a padlock where both people have the same key.
Quantum computers threaten current crypto: Shor's algorithm factors large numbers, breaking RSA/ECC.
